title: Quiz: Gradient Computation
description: Test your understanding of quantum gradient computation techniques

questions.question: For a gate G(theta) = exp(-i*theta*P/2) where P^2 = I, what shift value 's' is used in the standard parameter-shift rule?

options.text: pi/4
pi/2
pi
2*pi

questions.explanation: For gates with generators satisfying P^2 = I (like Pauli rotations), the shift s = pi/2 gives the exact gradient: dE/dtheta = [E(theta + pi/2) - E(theta - pi/2)] / 2.

questions.question: How many circuit evaluations are needed to compute the gradient of one parameter using the parameter-shift rule?
questions.explanation: The parameter-shift rule requires exactly 2 circuit evaluations per parameter: one at theta + s and one at theta - s. The gradient is computed from their difference.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of the parameter-shift rule over finite differences for quantum circuits?

options.text: It requires fewer circuit evaluations
It provides exact gradients (up to shot noise) without truncation error
It works for any type of gate
It eliminates shot noise completely

questions.explanation: The parameter-shift rule gives mathematically exact gradients for applicable gates. Finite differences always have truncation error, and small step sizes amplify shot noise. Both methods still have shot noise, but parameter-shift avoids truncation error.

questions.question: When does the generalized parameter-shift rule become necessary?

options.text: When the circuit has more than 10 qubits
When the gate generator has more than two distinct eigenvalues
When using noisy quantum hardware
When computing second derivatives

questions.explanation: The standard two-term parameter-shift rule works for generators with exactly two distinct eigenvalues (like Pauli matrices). For generators with more eigenvalues, the generalized rule with multiple shift terms is needed.

questions.question: Simultaneous measurement of commuting Pauli terms reduces the total number of circuit evaluations needed for gradient estimation.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. When Pauli terms commute, they can be measured together in a single circuit execution. Grouping commuting terms reduces the number of distinct measurement circuits needed to estimate the full Hamiltonian expectation value and its gradients.

questions.question: How many circuit evaluations are needed to compute a single off-diagonal Hessian element H_ij (i != j) using parameter-shift?
questions.explanation: Computing H_ij requires evaluating E at four points: (theta_i + s, theta_j + s), (theta_i + s, theta_j - s), (theta_i - s, theta_j + s), and (theta_i - s, theta_j - s). These four evaluations give the mixed partial derivative.

questions.question: What does the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) matrix measure?

options.text: The energy landscape curvature
The sensitivity of the quantum state to parameter changes
The amount of entanglement in the state
The number of gates in the circuit

questions.explanation: The QFI matrix measures how distinguishable quantum states become under infinitesimal parameter changes. It captures the geometry of quantum state space and is used in quantum natural gradient to account for parameter-space curvature.

questions.question: Which statements about finite difference gradient estimation are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Smaller step sizes always give more accurate gradients
Shot noise is amplified when step size is small
There is a bias-variance tradeoff in choosing step size
It can be applied to any differentiable gate

questions.explanation: (a) False - very small steps amplify shot noise. (b) True - dividing by small epsilon amplifies noise. (c) True - large steps have truncation error (bias), small steps have noise amplification (variance). (d) True - finite differences work for any gate, unlike parameter-shift which requires specific generator properties.
